infobox.value: **A:** The Greens originated as a [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing "Left-wing") protest party in the 1980s but have since evolved into a centre-left party, especially after merging with [Alliance 90](/wiki/Alliance_90 "Alliance 90") in 1993.[[9]](#cite_note-9) | |

([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bündnis 90/Die Grünen* [[ˈbʏntnɪs ˈnɔʏntsɪç diː ˈɡʁyːnən]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-B%C3%BCndnis_90-Die_Gr%C3%BCnen.ogg "File:De-Bündnis 90-Die Grünen.ogg")), often simply referred to as **Greens**[[a]](#cite_note-abbr-10) ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Grüne* [[ˈɡʁyːnə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Gr%C3%BCne.ogg "File:De-Grüne.ogg")), is a [green](/wiki/Green_(politics) "Green (politics)") [political party in Germany](/wiki/Political_party_in_Germany "Political party in Germany").[[10]](#cite_note-Nordsieck-11) It was formed in 1993 by the merger of the Greens (formed in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") in 1980) and [Alliance 90](/wiki/Alliance_90 "Alliance 90") (formed in [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") in 1990). The Greens had itself merged with the [East German Green Party](/wiki/East_German_Green_Party "East German Green Party") after [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") in 1990.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

**Alliance 90/The Greens** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bündnis 90/Die Grünen* [[ˈbʏntnɪs ˈnɔʏntsɪç diː ˈɡʁyːnən]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-B%C3%BCndnis_90-Die_Gr%C3%BCnen.ogg "File:De-Bündnis 90-Die Grünen.ogg")), often simply referred to as **Greens**[[a]](#cite_note-abbr-10) ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Grüne* [[ˈɡʁyːnə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Gr%C3%BCne.ogg "File:De-Grüne.ogg")), is a [green](/wiki/Green_(politics) "Green (politics)") [political party in Germany](/wiki/Political_party_in_Germany "Political party in Germany").[[10]](#cite_note-Nordsieck-11) It was formed in 1993 by the merger of the Greens (formed in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") in 1980) and [Alliance 90](/wiki/Alliance_90 "Alliance 90") (formed in [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") in 1990). The Greens had itself merged with the [East German Green Party](/wiki/East_German_Green_Party "East German Green Party") after [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") in 1990.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Since November 2024, [Franziska Brantner](/wiki/Franziska_Brantner "Franziska Brantner") and [Felix Banaszak](/wiki/Felix_Banaszak "Felix Banaszak") have been co-leaders of the party. It currently holds 85 of the 630 seats in the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag"), having won 11% of first votes and 11.6% of second votes cast in the [2025 federal election](/wiki/2025_German_federal_election "2025 German federal election"), putting it in fourth place of the seven political parties by number of seats.[[12]](#cite_note-21st_Bundestag_election_2025,_Germany:_Final_result-13) Its parliamentary co-leaders are [Britta Haßelmann](/wiki/Britta_Ha%C3%9Felmann "Britta Haßelmann") and [Katharina Dröge](/wiki/Katharina_Dr%C3%B6ge "Katharina Dröge"). The Greens have been part of the federal government twice: first as a junior partner to the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD) from 1998 to 2005, and then with the [SPD](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and the [Free Democratic Party](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)") (FDP) in the [traffic light coalition](/wiki/Traffic_light_coalition "Traffic light coalition") from the 2021 election until that coalition's collapse in 2024. In the [Scholz cabinet](/wiki/Scholz_cabinet "Scholz cabinet"), the Greens had five ministers, including Vice-Chancellor [Robert Habeck](/wiki/Robert_Habeck "Robert Habeck") and Foreign Minister [Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock").

The party holds seats in most of Germany's state legislatures, except [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland"), [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia") and [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), and is a member of coalition governments in seven states. [Winfried Kretschmann](/wiki/Winfried_Kretschmann "Winfried Kretschmann"), [Minister-President of Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/List_of_Ministers-President_of_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "List of Ministers-President of Baden-Württemberg"), is the only Green head of government in Germany. The [Landtag of Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Landtag_of_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Landtag of Baden-Württemberg") is also the only state legislature in which Alliance 90/The Greens is the largest party; it is the second largest party in the legislatures of [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein").

Alliance 90/The Greens is a founding member of the [European Green Party](/wiki/European_Green_Party "European Green Party") and the [Greens–European Free Alliance](/wiki/Greens%E2%80%93European_Free_Alliance "Greens–European Free Alliance") group in the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"). It is currently the largest party in the G/EFA group, with 21 [MEPs](/wiki/MEPs "MEPs"). In the [2019 European election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_Germany "2019 European Parliament election in Germany"), Alliance 90/The Greens was the second largest party in Germany, winning 20.5% of votes cast. The party had 166,000 members in February 2025, making it the third largest party in Germany by membership.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

The Green Party was initially founded in West Germany as *Die Grünen* (the Greens) in January 1980. It grew out of the [anti-nuclear energy](/wiki/Anti-nuclear_movement_in_Germany "Anti-nuclear movement in Germany"), environmental, peace, [new left](/wiki/New_left "New left"), and new social movements of the late 20th century.[[13]](#cite_note-14) More specifically, social and political movements that contributed to the milieu that produced the Greens included protests during the 1970s against [Wyhl](/wiki/Wyhl "Wyhl") and [Brokdorf Nuclear Power Plants](/wiki/Brokdorf_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Brokdorf Nuclear Power Plant"), the nuclear waste processing facility at [Gorleben](/wiki/Gorleben "Gorleben") and the construction of a new runway at [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport"), and the grassroots civic [Citizens' Initiative](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerinitiative "de:Bürgerinitiative") movement. Alternative left groups that flourished after the SPD abandoned Marxism, entered a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition_(Germany) "Grand coalition (Germany)") with the [CDU/CSU](/wiki/CDU/CSU "CDU/CSU") under Christian Democratic chancellor [Kurt Georg Kiesinger](/wiki/Kurt_Georg_Kiesinger "Kurt Georg Kiesinger"), and later [banned radical activists from being employed as civil servants](/wiki/Berufsverbot "Berufsverbot"), also formed a movement that contributed to the party's formation, including the [Außerparlamentarische Opposition](/wiki/Au%C3%9Ferparlamentarische_Opposition "Außerparlamentarische Opposition"), the [West German student movement](/wiki/West_German_student_movement "West German student movement"), the [K-Groups](/wiki/K-Groups_(Germany) "K-Groups (Germany)") and the [Spontis](/wiki/Spontis "Spontis"), as did a broader alternative subculture that included collectives, cooperatives, communes, self-help groups, [new religious movements](/wiki/New_religious_movement "New religious movement") and cultural organisations.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-bondemann-16)

Between 1977 and 1979 local parties running on on environmental and peace issues were established across West Germany. Although most environmental and anti-nuclear activists were initially sceptical of these efforts, a lack of results from [pressure group](/wiki/Pressure_group "Pressure group") activity and early electoral successes meant the approach gained momentum and led to local groups amalgamating into state-level parties.[[16]](#cite_note-parkin118-17)

*Grüne Liste Umweltschutz* (GLU - green list for environmental protection) was the name used for some branches in [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") and other states in the Federal Republic of Germany, including Hamburg, [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"), [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") and [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Alliance_90/The_Greens_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Alliance 90/The Greens Baden-Württemberg").[[16]](#cite_note-parkin118-17) These groups were founded in 1977 and took part in several elections. Most GLU activists had been involved in [environmental protection](/wiki/Environmental_protection "Environmental protection") activism, although it increasingly also attracted campaigners on social issues.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Most GLU branches merged with The Greens in 1980.

The [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") state branch of The Greens was founded as *Alternative Liste*, or precisely, *Alternative Liste für Demokratie und Umweltschutz* (AL; alternative list for democracy and environmental protection) in 1978 and became the official West Berlin branch of The Greens in 1980. In 1993, it renamed to Alliance 90/The Greens Berlin after the merger with East Berlin's Greens and Alliance 90.

The [Hamburg state branch](/wiki/Alliance_90/The_Greens_Hamburg "Alliance 90/The Greens Hamburg") of the Green Party was called *Grün-Alternative Liste Hamburg* (GAL; green-alternative list) from its foundation in 1982 until 2012. In 1984, it became the official Hamburg branch of The Greens.

In July 1978 former Christian Democrat [Herbert Gruhl](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herbert_Gruhl "de:Herbert Gruhl") founded [*Grüne Aktion Zukunft*](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%BCne_Aktion_Zukunft "de:Grüne Aktion Zukunft") (GAZ - green action future) as the beginning of a process to establish a nationwide green party. Among the groups expressing interest in this were the [*Bundesverband Bürgerinitiativen Umweltschutz*](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bundesverband_B%C3%BCrgerinitiativen_Umweltschutz "de:Bundesverband Bürgerinitiativen Umweltschutz") (BBU - federal association of citizen's initiatives for environmental protection), a movement of citizen's initiative groups focused on anti-nuclear and environmental issues, members of the [anthroposophy](/wiki/Anthroposophy "Anthroposophy") movement, the [Free International University](/wiki/Free_International_University "Free International University"), Spontis and disaffected Social Democrats.[[18]](#cite_note-parkin119-19)

In February 1979 an electoral alliance was established to contest [the first democratic elections to the European Parliament later that year](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election_in_West_Germany "1979 European Parliament election in West Germany") under the name *Sonstige Politische Vereinigung Die Grünen* (SPV-Die Grünen - alternative political alliance, the greens).[[18]](#cite_note-parkin119-19) This included GAZ, GLU, [*Grüne Liste Schleswig-Holstein*](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%BCne_Liste_Schleswig-Holstein "de:Grüne Liste Schleswig-Holstein") and the [Action Group of Independent Germans](/wiki/Action_Group_of_Independent_Germans "Action Group of Independent Germans") (a pro-[neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country") pacifist political party which had adopted pro-environment policies in the 1970s and was predominantly present in southern Germany).[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-parkin119-19) Two members of the BBU's executive, [Petra Kelly](/wiki/Petra_Kelly "Petra Kelly") and [Roland Vogt](/wiki/Roland_Vogt "Roland Vogt"), also joined the alliance, and it was also supported by prominent writer [Heinrich Böll](/wiki/Heinrich_B%C3%B6ll "Heinrich Böll") and socialist activist [Rudi Dutschke](/wiki/Rudi_Dutschke "Rudi Dutschke"). However, not all local green groups supported the coalition, including the West Berlin *[Alternative Liste](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%BCndnis_90/Die_Gr%C3%BCnen_Berlin "de:Bündnis 90/Die Grünen Berlin")* and the *Bunte Liste* (Multicoloured List) in Hamburg, the latter being a rival green party to the city's GLU branch. Both of these lists were heavily influenced by the activism of the [Communist League](/wiki/Communist_League_(West_Germany) "Communist League (West Germany)"). SPV-Die Grünen ran on a platform advocating a [nuclear-free Europe](/wiki/Nuclear-free_Europe "Nuclear-free Europe") and a [Europe of the Regions](/wiki/Europe_of_the_Regions "Europe of the Regions"), and scored 3.2 percent of the vote.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

As German electoral law provided for a generous scheme for reimbursement of [election expenses](/wiki/Election_expenses "Election expenses"), providing them to all parties scoring at least 0.5 percent of the vote, the new formation netted 4.8 million deutschmarks, after spending just 300,000 on its campaign. This money was spent on establishing state and national party infrastructure. SPV-Die Grünen's success led to increased activist interest, with membership rising to around 10,000 by the end of 1979, and the Alternative and Multicoloured Lists showing more interest in joining forces with the other green groups.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

A congress in October 1979 to agree a programme for the national party led to heated disagreements over economic orientation and non-violence and whether dual membership with other parties was to be permitted. Whilst the latter was resolved by agreeing to devolve decisions on membership to local branches, the former was overcome by the adoption of what would become known as the four pillars of green politics: ecology, social responsibility, grassroots democracy and non-violence. Journalist and activist [August Haußleiter](/wiki/August_Hau%C3%9Fleiter "August Haußleiter") claimed to have proposed these and secured the agreement of the leaders of the right (Gruhl) and left ([Jürgen Reents](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Reents "Jürgen Reents")) factions at the conference.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The nascent movement received a further boost the same month when the [*Bremer Grüne Liste*](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bremer_Gr%C3%BCne_Liste "de:Bremer Grüne Liste") became the first green party to win seats in a state parliament, securing four seats in [Bremen's election](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerschaftswahl_in_Bremen_1979 "de:Bürgerschaftswahl in Bremen 1979").[[23]](#cite_note-parkin121-24)

### 12–13 January 1980: Foundation congress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 12–13 January 1980: Foundation congress")]

The political party **The Greens** (German: *Die Grünen*) sprung out of the wave of [New Social Movements](/wiki/New_social_movements "New social movements") that were active in the 1970s, including [environmentalist](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement"), [anti-war](/wiki/Anti-war_movement "Anti-war movement"), and [anti-nuclear](/wiki/Anti-nuclear_movement "Anti-nuclear movement") movements which can trace their origin to the [student protests of 1968](/wiki/Protests_of_1968 "Protests of 1968"). Officially founded as a German national party on 13 January 1980 in [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe"), the party sought to give these movements political and parliamentary representation, as the pre-existing [people's parties](/wiki/People%27s_party_(politics) "People's party (politics)") were not organised in a way to address their stated issues.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Its membership included organisers from former attempts to achieve institutional representation such as [GLU](/w/index.php?title=Gr%C3%BCne_Liste_Umweltschutz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grüne Liste Umweltschutz (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%BCne_Liste_Umweltschutz "de:Grüne Liste Umweltschutz")] and [AUD](/wiki/Action_Group_of_Independent_Germans "Action Group of Independent Germans"). Opposition to pollution, use of [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") military action, and certain aspects of [industrialised society](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") were principal campaign issues.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The party also championed [sexual liberation](/wiki/Sexual_revolution "Sexual revolution") and some of their members supported the abolition of [age-of-consent laws](/wiki/Age-of-consent_reform "Age-of-consent reform").[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The formation of a party was purportedly first discussed by movement leaders in 1978. Important figures in the first years were – among others – [Petra Kelly](/wiki/Petra_Kelly "Petra Kelly"), [Joschka Fischer](/wiki/Joschka_Fischer "Joschka Fischer"), [Gert Bastian](/wiki/Gert_Bastian "Gert Bastian"), [Lukas Beckmann](/w/index.php?title=Lukas_Beckmann&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lukas Beckmann (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lukas_Beckmann "de:Lukas Beckmann")], [Rudolf Bahro](/wiki/Rudolf_Bahro "Rudolf Bahro"), [Joseph Beuys](/wiki/Joseph_Beuys "Joseph Beuys"), [Antje Vollmer](/wiki/Antje_Vollmer "Antje Vollmer"), [Herbert Gruhl](/w/index.php?title=Herbert_Gruhl&action=edit&redlink=1 "Herbert Gruhl (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herbert_Gruhl "de:Herbert Gruhl")], [August Haußleiter](/wiki/August_Hau%C3%9Fleiter "August Haußleiter"), [Luise Rinser](/wiki/Luise_Rinser "Luise Rinser"), [Dirk Schneider](/w/index.php?title=Dirk_Schneider_(politician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dirk Schneider (politician) (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirk_Schneider_(politician) "de:Dirk Schneider (politician)")], [Christian Ströbele](/wiki/Christian_Str%C3%B6bele "Christian Ströbele"), [Jutta Ditfurth](/wiki/Jutta_Ditfurth "Jutta Ditfurth"), [Baldur Springmann](/wiki/Baldur_Springmann "Baldur Springmann") and [Werner Vogel](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Werner_Vogel_(Politiker) "de:Werner Vogel (Politiker)").

In the foundational [congress of 1980](https://bnn.de/lokales/karlsruhe/gruendungsparteitag-der-gruenen-1980-in-karlsruhe-das-war-eine-ziemlich-schwere-geburt), the ideological tenets of the party were consolidated, proclaiming the famous Four Pillars of the Green Party:

* [Social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice")
* [Ecological wisdom](/wiki/Ecological_wisdom "Ecological wisdom")
* [Grassroots democracy](/wiki/Grassroots_democracy "Grassroots democracy")
* [Nonviolence](/wiki/Nonviolence "Nonviolence")

### 1980s: Parliamentary representation on the federal level

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s: Parliamentary representation on the federal level")]

As the 1980s commenced, the movement built on their success in the 1979 Bremen state elections, winning six seats in the [1980 Baden-Württemberg state election](/wiki/1980_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_state_election "1980 Baden-Württemberg state election") and nine seats in the [1981 West Berlin state election](/wiki/1981_West_Berlin_state_election "1981 West Berlin state election"), although the result in the [1980 West German federal election](/wiki/1980_West_German_federal_election "1980 West German federal election") was disappointing, with only 1.5 percent of the vote scored.[[23]](#cite_note-parkin121-24) In 1982, the conservative factions of the Greens broke away to form the [Ecological Democratic Party](/wiki/Ecological_Democratic_Party "Ecological Democratic Party") (ÖDP). Those who remained in the Green party were more strongly [pacifist](/wiki/Pacifism "Pacifism") and against restrictions on immigration and [reproductive rights](/wiki/Reproductive_rights "Reproductive rights"), while supporting the legalisation of [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") use, placing a higher priority on working for [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Germany "LGBT rights in Germany"), and tending to advocate what they described as "anti-authoritarian" concepts of education and child-rearing. They also tended to identify more closely with a culture of protest and [civil disobedience](/wiki/Civil_disobedience "Civil disobedience"), frequently clashing with police at demonstrations against [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons "Nuclear weapons"), [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power"), and the construction of a new runway (*Startbahn West*) at [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport"). Those who left the party at the time might have felt similarly about some of these issues, but did not identify with the forms of protest that Green party members took part in.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After some success at [state-level](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") elections, winning 11 seats in [Lower Saxony](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landtagswahl_in_Niedersachsen_1982 "de:Landtagswahl in Niedersachsen 1982"), nine seats in [Hesse](/wiki/1982_Hessian_state_election "1982 Hessian state election") and eight seats in [Hamburg](/wiki/December_1982_Hamburg_state_election "December 1982 Hamburg state election") during the course of 1982, the party won 27 seats (plus one non-voting delegate from West Berlin) with 5.7% of the vote in the Bundestag, the lower house of the German parliament, in the [1983 federal election](/wiki/1983_West_German_federal_election "1983 West German federal election").[[23]](#cite_note-parkin121-24) Among the important political issues at the time was the deployment of [Pershing II](/wiki/Pershing_II "Pershing II") [IRBMs](/wiki/Intermediate-range_ballistic_missile "Intermediate-range ballistic missile") and nuclear-tipped [cruise missiles](/wiki/Cruise_missile "Cruise missile") by the U.S. and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") on West German soil, generating strong opposition in the general population that found an outlet in mass demonstrations. The newly formed party was able to draw on this popular movement to recruit support.

The state electoral success in Hesse and Hamburg saw the party holding the [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(parliament) "Balance of power (parliament)") in both states. In Hamburg the party won nine seats in the [June 1982 election](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerschaftswahl_in_Hamburg_1982_(Juni) "de:Bürgerschaftswahl in Hamburg 1982 (Juni)") and made an offer of terms for [confidence and supply](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") to the Social Democrats, but these were rejected. At the following December election the Greens lost a seat and the SPD won an absolute majority. In Hesse the Greens rejected the idea of cooperating with the SPD after the 1982 election. [Another state election took place there the following year](/wiki/1983_Hessian_state_election "1983 Hessian state election"), resulting in the Greens losing two seats but no party achieving a majority. Following the election the state Greens voted to accept a confidence and supply agreement with the Social Democrats.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Although this was implemented in 1984 after long negotiations between the Greens and the SPD, with the former voting in favour of [Holger Börner](/wiki/Holger_B%C3%B6rner "Holger Börner") as minister-president and the SPD budget in June, the agreement broke down in December over subsidising the expansion of the [Nukem](/wiki/Nukem_Technologies "Nukem Technologies") / Alkem nuclear enrichment plant in [Hanau](/wiki/Hanau "Hanau"). Despite this negotiations immediately commenced over terms for a coalition agreement.[[27]](#cite_note-parkin132-28)

Although the [party's Bundestag faction](/wiki/Green_Party_faction_(Bundestag) "Green Party faction (Bundestag)") had limited success in legislating during the new MPs' first term, they were able to influence debate inside and outside parliament through proposing bills and asking questions. In addition, [Otto Schily](/wiki/Otto_Schily "Otto Schily")'s work as part of the parliamentary committee investigating the [Flick affair](/wiki/Flick_affair "Flick affair"), concerning illicit payments to the established German parliamentary parties by businessman [Friedrich Karl Flick](/wiki/Friedrich_Karl_Flick "Friedrich Karl Flick"), was particularly notable, pressuring SPD colleagues to refrain from [whitewashing](/wiki/Whitewashing_(communications) "Whitewashing (communications)") the matter and scuppering an attempt by the [Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl") government to pass an amnesty for those involved. However, the faction's efforts were disrupted by the attempt to implement rotation of members halfway through their terms as had been planned before the 1983 election. All elected Bundestag members except Petra Kelly stepped down to enable rotation, however of the seven Green MEPs elected in the [1984 European election](/wiki/1984_European_Parliament_election_in_West_Germany "1984 European Parliament election in West Germany") only three resigned from their roles. The rotation stipulation was subsequently abolished, however the departure of many Bundestag members who had received significant media attention upon their entry to parliament led to reduced press interest in the parliamentary faction's work.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Progress was made in elections in 1984. As well as entering the European Parliament for the first time, the party increased their share of the vote from 5.3 to eight percent and took a total of nine seats in the [1984 Baden-Württemberg state election](/wiki/1984_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_state_election "1984 Baden-Württemberg state election"), whilst in local elections it exceeded the five percent electoral threshold for the first time in [Saarland](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kommunalwahlen_im_Saarland_1984 "de:Kommunalwahlen im Saarland 1984") and [Rhineland-Palatinate](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kommunalwahlrecht_(Rheinland-Pfalz) "de:Kommunalwahlrecht (Rheinland-Pfalz)") and scored over eight percent in [North Rhine-Westphalia](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ergebnisse_der_Kommunalwahlen_in_Nordrhein-Westfalen_ab_1975 "de:Ergebnisse der Kommunalwahlen in Nordrhein-Westfalen ab 1975").[[27]](#cite_note-parkin132-28)

However, elections in 1985 brought mixed results. In the [Saarland state election](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landtagswahl_im_Saarland_1985 "de:Landtagswahl im Saarland 1985"), SPD leader [Oskar Lafontaine](/wiki/Oskar_Lafontaine "Oskar Lafontaine") ran a campaign emphasising both socio-economic and environmental issues, declaring that "progress has a name... it is called eco-socialism", and led the party to 49.2 percent of the vote and an outright majority in the chamber for the first time, whilst the Greens could only muster 2.5 percent. Contrastingly, in the [West Berlin elections](/wiki/1985_West_Berlin_state_election "1985 West Berlin state election"), after the SPD rejected the Alternative List's terms for a confidence and supply agreement before polling day, the SPD scored their worst post-war result of 32.4 percent, whilst the Greens almost doubled their vote to 10.6 percent and took 15 seats. In the [North Rhine-Westphalia state election](/wiki/1985_North_Rhine-Westphalia_state_election "1985 North Rhine-Westphalia state election"), SPD leader [Johannes Rau](/wiki/Johannes_Rau "Johannes Rau") ran a campaign opposing cooperation with the Greens, whose campaign was hampered by two controversies, namely the inclusion of a proposal to abolish the age of consent in the state party's manifesto (which was subsequently removed by a hastily-called party congress) and the sending of a letter of outreach by federal MPs [Antje Vollmer](/wiki/Antje_Vollmer "Antje Vollmer") and [Christa Nickels](/wiki/Christa_Nickels "Christa Nickels") to [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction") members who were on hunger strike in prison. Whilst the SPD won 52.1 percent of the vote, the Greens failed to meet the electoral threshold with 4.6 percent.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Meanwhile, in [local elections in Hesse](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kommunalwahlen_in_Hessen_1985 "de:Kommunalwahlen in Hessen 1985") both the SPD and Greens made gains, with the vote of the latter rising to 7.1 percent. Shortly after Börner offered a coalition deal to the Greens which included the position of [environment and energy minister](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hessisches_Ministerium_f%C3%BCr_Landwirtschaft_und_Umwelt,_Weinbau,_Forsten,_Jagd_und_Heimat "de:Hessisches Ministerium für Landwirtschaft und Umwelt, Weinbau, Forsten, Jagd und Heimat"). The Greens were divided about accepting the offer, particularly after a demonstrator, Günter Sarre, was killed by police at an [anti-fascist](/wiki/Antifa_(Germany) "Antifa (Germany)") protest in Hesse's largest city, [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") in September: the state party accepted the coalition offer shortly afterwards, prompting a denunciation by [Jutta Ditfurth](/wiki/Jutta_Ditfurth "Jutta Ditfurth") on behalf of the federal party. [Joschka Fischer](/wiki/Joschka_Fischer "Joschka Fischer") took up the ministerial post, whilst [Marita Haibach](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marita_Haibach "de:Marita Haibach") was made [secretary of state](/wiki/Secretary_of_state "Secretary of state") for woman's affairs. At the federal party conference in [Offenburg](/wiki/Offenburg "Offenburg") in December, the party's [fundi](/wiki/Fundi_(politics) "Fundi (politics)") faction, which had been hostile to the coalition agreement, took control of the party's executive.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In 1986, the intra-party conflict reduced somewhat. A party conference in [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover") that Easter voted for an immediate end to nuclear power and the end of the coalition in Hesse. In [that year's Lower Saxony state election](/wiki/1986_Lower_Saxony_state_election "1986 Lower Saxony state election"), the governing Christian Democrats mounted a [smear campaign](/wiki/Smear_campaign "Smear campaign") against the Greens, releasing details about party officials from police records, caricaturing party policy, and claiming that they were [foreign agents](/wiki/Foreign_agent "Foreign agent") of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and that the movement had both political and paramilitary wings along the lines of [ETA](/wiki/ETA_(separatist_group) "ETA (separatist group)"). These attacks limited the party's gains to 0.6 percent of the vote, failing to add to the 11 seats won at the previous state election. However, in the [Bavarian state election](/wiki/1986_Bavarian_state_election "1986 Bavarian state election") the party entered parliament for the first time, winning 7.5 percent of the vote and 15 seats, whilst in the [Hamburg elections](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerschaftswahl_in_Hamburg_1986 "de:Bürgerschaftswahl in Hamburg 1986") an all-female list secured 10.4 percent of votes and 13 seats, whilst neither the CDU or SPD secured a parliamentary majority.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

Partly due to the impact of the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") in 1986, and to growing awareness of the threat of air pollution and [acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain") to German forests (*[Waldsterben](/wiki/Forest_dieback "Forest dieback")*), the Greens increased their share of the vote to 8.3% in the [1987 federal election](/wiki/1987_West_German_federal_election "1987 West German federal election"), winning 42 seats plus two non-voting West Berlin delegates.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Around this time, [Joschka Fischer](/wiki/Joschka_Fischer "Joschka Fischer") emerged as the unofficial leader of the party, which he remained until resigning all leadership posts following the [2005 federal election](/wiki/2005_German_federal_election "2005 German federal election").

Following the federal election, factional tensions over strategy re-emerged. The Hesse party withdrew from their coalition with the SPD, over the same nuclear enrichment plant which had led to the collapse of the previous confidence and supply agreement. At the subsequent [1987 Hessian state election](/wiki/1987_Hessian_state_election "1987 Hessian state election"), the SPD vote declined, leading to a coalition of Christian Democrats and Free Democrats entering government, but the Green vote increased to 9.4 percent, securing them 10 seats. In the [Hamburg election](/wiki/1987_Hamburg_state_election "1987 Hamburg state election") held to resolve the deadlock resulting from the previous year's poll, the Green vote dropped to 7.1 percent, losing them five seats, whilst the SPD and FDP were able to form a coalition. Meanwhile in [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/1987_Rhineland-Palatinate_state_election "1987 Rhineland-Palatinate state election") the Greens entered parliament for the first time, taking 5.9 percent of the vote and six seats. Later that year in [state elections in Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/1987_Schleswig-Holstein_state_election "1987 Schleswig-Holstein state election") the state Green party, which was opposed to participating in any coalition, failed to enter parliament and won only 3.9 percent of the vote, whilst in [Bremen](/wiki/1987_Bremen_state_election "1987 Bremen state election") they almost doubled their vote to 10.2 percent and won ten seats.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Tensions were further inflamed by the killing of two police officers and injury of nine others by [autonomist](/wiki/Autonomist_Marxism "Autonomist Marxism") activists at a protest at Frankfurt Airport. Whilst the bulk of the party condemned the killings, some on the left including Ditfurth and [Thomas Ebermann](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Ebermann "de:Thomas Ebermann") did not. The growing disagreements at the federal level resulted in a crisis meeting in December 1987, following by a perspective congress in June 1988.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The Greens were the target of attempts by the East German secret police to enlist the cooperation of members who were willing to align the party with the agenda of the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic"). The party ranks included several politicians who were later discovered to have been [Stasi](/wiki/Stasi "Stasi") agents, including [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag") representative Dirk Schneider, [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") representative [Brigitte Heinrich](/wiki/Brigitte_Heinrich "Brigitte Heinrich"), and [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction") defense lawyer [Klaus Croissant](/wiki/Klaus_Croissant "Klaus Croissant"). Greens politician and Bundestag representative [Gert Bastian](/wiki/Gert_Bastian "Gert Bastian") was also a founding member of [Generals for Peace](/w/index.php?title=Generals_for_Peace&action=edit&redlink=1 "Generals for Peace (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generale_f%C3%BCr_den_Frieden "de:Generale für den Frieden")], a pacifist group created and funded by the Stasi, the revelation of which may have contributed to the murder-suicide in which he killed his partner and Greens founder [Petra Kelly](/wiki/Petra_Kelly "Petra Kelly").[[35]](#cite_note-36) A study commissioned by the Greens determined that 15 to 20 members intimately cooperated with the Stasi and another 450 to 500 had been informants.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Until 1987, the Greens included a faction involved in [pedophile activism](/wiki/Pedophile_activism "Pedophile activism"), the *SchwuP* short for *Arbeitsgemeinschaft "Schwule, Päderasten und Transsexuelle"* (approx. *working group "Gays, Pederasts and Transsexuals"*). This faction campaigned for repealing § 176 of the German penal code, dealing with [child sexual abuse](/wiki/Child_sexual_abuse "Child sexual abuse"). This group was controversial within the party itself, and was seen as partly responsible for the poor election result of 1985.[[38]](#cite_note-39) [This controversy re-surfaced in 2013](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/P%C3%A4dophilie-Debatte_(B%C3%BCndnis_90/Die_Gr%C3%BCnen) "de:Pädophilie-Debatte (Bündnis 90/Die Grünen)") and chairwoman [Claudia Roth](/wiki/Claudia_Roth "Claudia Roth") stated she welcomed an independent scientific investigation on the extent of influence pedophile activists had on the party in the mid-1980s.[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41) In November 2014, the political scientist Franz Walter presented the final report about his research on a press conference.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### 1990s: German reunification, electoral failure in the West, formation of Alliance 90/The Greens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s: German reunification, electoral failure in the West, formation of Alliance 90/The Greens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-1990-0208-015%2C_Logo_Gr%C3%BCne_Partei_der_DDR.svg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-1990-0208-015%2C_Logo_Gr%C3%BCne_Partei_der_DDR.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-1990-0208-015,_Logo_Gr%C3%BCne_Partei_der_DDR.svg)

The 1990 [GDR's Green Party](/wiki/East_German_Green_Party "East German Green Party") logo

In the [1990 federal elections](/wiki/1990_German_federal_election "1990 German federal election"), taking place post-[reunified Germany](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification"), the Greens in the West did not pass the 5% limit required to win seats in the Bundestag. It was only due to a temporary modification of German election law, applying the five-percent "hurdle" separately in East and West Germany, that the Greens acquired any parliamentary seats at all. This happened because in the [new states of Germany](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany"), the Greens, in a joint effort with [Alliance 90](/wiki/Alliance_90 "Alliance 90"), a heterogeneous grouping of [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_and_political_rights "Civil and political rights") activists, were able to gain more than 5% of the vote. Some critics attribute this poor performance to the reluctance of the campaign to cater to the prevalent mood of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism"), instead focusing on subjects such as [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"). A campaign poster at the time proudly stated, "Everyone is talking about Germany; we're talking about the weather!", paraphrasing a popular slogan of [Deutsche Bundesbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbahn "Deutsche Bundesbahn"), the German national railway. The party also opposed imminent reunification that was in process, instead wanting to initiate debates on ecology and nuclear issues before reunification causing a drop in support in Western Germany.[[42]](#cite_note-43) After the [1994 federal election](/wiki/1994_German_federal_election "1994 German federal election"); however, the merged party returned to the Bundestag, and the Greens received 7.3% of the vote nationwide and 49 seats.

### 1998–2002: Greens as governing party, first term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1998–2002: Greens as governing party, first term")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Gruenen_Velotaxi_20050813.jpg/250px-Gruenen_Velotaxi_20050813.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gruenen_Velotaxi_20050813.jpg)

A [cycle rickshaw](/wiki/Cycle_rickshaw "Cycle rickshaw") (velotaxi) in front of the German Bundestag in Berlin with the Alliance 90/The Greens livery for the [2005 federal election](/wiki/2005_German_federal_election "2005 German federal election")

In the [1998 federal election](/wiki/1998_German_federal_election "1998 German federal election"), despite a slight fall in their percentage of the vote (6.7%), the Greens retained 47 seats and joined the federal government for the first time in '[Red-Green](/wiki/Red%E2%80%93green_alliance "Red–green alliance")' [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") with the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD). [Joschka Fischer](/wiki/Joschka_Fischer "Joschka Fischer") became [Vice-Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Vice-Chancellor_of_Germany "Vice-Chancellor of Germany") and [foreign minister](/wiki/Foreign_Minister_of_Germany "Foreign Minister of Germany") in the new government, which had two other Green ministers ([Andrea Fischer](/wiki/Andrea_Fischer "Andrea Fischer"), later [Renate Künast](/wiki/Renate_K%C3%BCnast "Renate Künast"), and [Jürgen Trittin](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Trittin "Jürgen Trittin")).

Almost immediately the party was plunged into a crisis by the question of German participation in the [NATO actions](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War") in Kosovo. Numerous anti-war party members resigned their party membership when the first post-war deployment of German troops in a military conflict abroad occurred under a Red-Green government, and the party began to experience a long string of defeats in local and state-level elections. Disappointment with the Green participation in government increased when anti-nuclear power activists realised that shutting down the nation's nuclear power stations would not happen as quickly as they wished, and numerous pro-business SPD members of the federal cabinet opposed the environmentalist agenda of the Greens, calling for tacit compromises.

In 2001, the party experienced a further crisis as some Green Members of Parliament refused to back the government's plan of sending military personnel to help with the [2001 invasion of Afghanistan](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom"). [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") [Gerhard Schröder](/wiki/Gerhard_Schr%C3%B6der "Gerhard Schröder") called a vote of confidence, tying it to his strategy on the war. Four Green MPs and one Social Democrat voted against the government, but Schröder was still able to command a majority.

On the other hand, the Greens achieved a major success as a governing party through the 2000 decision to phase out the use of nuclear energy. [Minister of Environment, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety](/wiki/Federal_Ministry_for_Environment,_Nature_Conservation_and_Nuclear_Safety "Federal Ministry for Environment, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety") Jürgen Trittin reached an agreement with energy companies on the gradual phasing out of [the country's nineteen nuclear power plants](/wiki/List_of_nuclear_reactors#Germany "List of nuclear reactors") and a cessation of civil usage of nuclear power by 2020. This was authorised through the [Nuclear Exit Law](/w/index.php?title=Nuclear_Exit_Law&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nuclear Exit Law (page does not exist)"). Based on an estimate of 32 years as the normal period of operation for a nuclear power plant, the agreement defines precisely how much energy a power plant is allowed to produce before being shut down. This law has since been overturned.

### 2002–2005: Greens as governing party, second term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2002–2005: Greens as governing party, second term")]

Despite the crises of the preceding electoral period, in the [2002 federal election](/wiki/2002_German_federal_election "2002 German federal election"), the Greens increased their total to 55 seats (in a smaller parliament) and 8.6%. This was partly due to the perception that the internal debate over the war in Afghanistan had been more honest and open than in other parties, and one of the MPs who had voted against the Afghanistan deployment, [Hans-Christian Ströbele](/wiki/Hans-Christian_Str%C3%B6bele "Hans-Christian Ströbele"), was directly elected to the Bundestag as a district representative for the [Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg – Prenzlauer Berg East constituency](/wiki/Berlin-Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg_%E2%80%93_Prenzlauer_Berg_East "Berlin-Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg – Prenzlauer Berg East") in Berlin, becoming the first Green to ever gain a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") seat in Germany.

The Greens benefited from increased inroads among traditionally left-wing demographics which had benefited from Green-initiated legislation in the 1998–2002 term, such as environmentalists ([Renewable Energies Act](/w/index.php?title=Renewable_Energies_Act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Renewable Energies Act (page does not exist)")) and LGBT groups ([Registered Partnership](/wiki/Registered_Partnership "Registered Partnership") Law). Perhaps most important for determining the success of both the Greens and the SPD was the increasing threat of war in Iraq, which was highly unpopular with the German public, and helped gather votes for the parties which had taken a stand against participation in this war. Despite losses for the SPD, the Red-Green coalition government retained a very slight majority in the Bundestag (4 seats) and was renewed, with [Joschka Fischer](/wiki/Joschka_Fischer "Joschka Fischer") as foreign minister, [Renate Künast](/wiki/Renate_K%C3%BCnast "Renate Künast") as minister for consumer protection, nutrition and agriculture, and [Jürgen Trittin](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Trittin "Jürgen Trittin") as minister for the environment.

One internal issue in 2002 was the failed attempt to settle a long-standing discussion about the question of whether members of parliament should be allowed to become members of the party executive. Two party conventions declined to change the party statute. The necessary majority of two-thirds was missed by a small margin. As a result, former party chairpersons [Fritz Kuhn](/wiki/Fritz_Kuhn "Fritz Kuhn") and [Claudia Roth](/wiki/Claudia_Roth "Claudia Roth") (who had been elected to parliament that year) were no longer able to continue in their executive function and were replaced by former party secretary general [Reinhard Bütikofer](/wiki/Reinhard_B%C3%BCtikofer "Reinhard Bütikofer") and former Bundestag member [Angelika Beer](/wiki/Angelika_Beer "Angelika Beer"). The party then held a member referendum on this question in the spring of 2003 which changed the party statute. Now members of parliament may be elected for two of the six seats of the party executive, as long as they are not ministers or caucus leaders. 57% of all party members voted in the member referendum, with 67% voting in favor of the change. The referendum was only the second in the history of Alliance 90/The Greens, the first having been held about the merger of the Greens and Alliance 90. In 2004, after Angelika Beer was elected to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"), Claudia Roth was elected to replace her as party chair.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Bdk-oldenburg-2005-kuenast.jpg/250px-Bdk-oldenburg-2005-kuenast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bdk-oldenburg-2005-kuenast.jpg)

Federal party convention in Oldenburg; Renate Künast speaking (2005)

The only party convention in 2003 was planned for November 2003, but about 20% of the local organisations forced the federal party to hold a special party convention in [Cottbus](/wiki/Cottbus "Cottbus") early to discuss the party position regarding *[Agenda 2010](/wiki/Agenda_2010 "Agenda 2010")*, a major reform of the German welfare programmes planned by Chancellor Schröder.

The November 2003 party convention was held in [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden") and decided the election platform for the 2004 [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") elections. The German Green list for these elections was headed by [Rebecca Harms](/wiki/Rebecca_Harms "Rebecca Harms") (then leader of the Green party in Lower Saxony) and [Daniel Cohn-Bendit](/wiki/Daniel_Cohn-Bendit "Daniel Cohn-Bendit"), previously Member of the European Parliament for [The Greens of France](/wiki/The_Greens_(France) "The Greens (France)"). The November 2003 convention is also noteworthy because it was the first convention of a German political party ever to use an [electronic voting](/wiki/Electronic_voting "Electronic voting") system.

The Greens gained a record 13 of Germany's 99 seats in these elections, mainly due to the perceived competence of Green ministers in the federal government and the unpopularity of the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany").

In early 2005, the Greens were the target of the [German Visa Affair 2005](/wiki/German_Visa_Affair_2005 "German Visa Affair 2005"), instigated in the media by the [Christian Democratic Union](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_Union_(Germany) "Christian Democratic Union (Germany)") (CDU). At the end of April 2005, they celebrated the decommissioning of the [Obrigheim nuclear power station](/wiki/Obrigheim_nuclear_power_station "Obrigheim nuclear power station"). They also continue to support a bill for an [Anti-Discrimination Law](/wiki/Anti-Discrimination_Law "Anti-Discrimination Law") ([Allgemeines Gleichbehandlungsgesetz](/w/index.php?title=Allgemeines_Gleichbehandlungsgesetz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Allgemeines Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allgemeines_Gleichbehandlungsgesetz "de:Allgemeines Gleichbehandlungsgesetz")]) in the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag").

In May 2005, the only remaining state-level [red-green coalition](/wiki/Red-green_coalition "Red-green coalition") government lost the vote in the [North Rhine-Westphalia state election](/wiki/2005_North_Rhine-Westphalia_state_election "2005 North Rhine-Westphalia state election"), leaving only the federal government with participation of the Greens (apart from local governments). In the early [2005 federal election](/wiki/2005_German_federal_election "2005 German federal election") the party incurred very small losses and achieved 8.1% of the vote and 51 seats. However, due to larger losses of the SPD, the previous coalition no longer had a majority in the Bundestag.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/National_Vote_map_die_Gruenen.JPG/250px-National_Vote_map_die_Gruenen.JPG)](/wiki/File:National_Vote_map_die_Gruenen.JPG)

Map showing Alliance 90/The Greens vote in each of the German constituencies at the [2005 German federal election](/wiki/2005_German_federal_election "2005 German federal election")

### 2005–2021: In opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2005–2021: In opposition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Bundestag_2009_Buendnisgruene.svg/250px-Bundestag_2009_Buendnisgruene.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bundestag_2009_Buendnisgruene.svg)

Map showing Alliance 90/The Greens vote in each of the German constituencies at the [2009 German federal election](/wiki/2009_German_federal_election "2009 German federal election")

For almost two years after the federal election in 2005, the Greens were not part of any government at the state or federal level. In June 2007, the Greens in [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen") entered into a coalition with the Social Democratic Party (SPD) following the [2007 Bremen state election](/wiki/2007_Bremen_state_election "2007 Bremen state election").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Greens_in_Government_G-Coordination.png/250px-Greens_in_Government_G-Coordination.png)](/wiki/File:Greens_in_Government_G-Coordination.png)

Professionalization of G-Coordination since 2007

In April 2008, following the [2008 Hamburg state election](/wiki/2008_Hamburg_state_election "2008 Hamburg state election"), the Green-Alternative List (GAL) in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") entered into a coalition with the Christian Democratic Union (CDU), the first [such](/w/index.php?title=Schwarz-gr%C3%BCne_Koalition&action=edit&redlink=1 "Schwarz-grüne Koalition (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwarz-gr%C3%BCne_Koalition "de:Schwarz-grüne Koalition")] state-level coalition in Germany. Although the GAL had to agree to the deepening of the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") River, the construction of a new [coal-fired power station](/wiki/Coal-fired_power_station "Coal-fired power station") and two road projects they had opposed, they also received some significant concessions from the CDU. These included reforming state schools by increasing the number of primary school [educational stages](/wiki/Educational_stage "Educational stage"), the restoration of trams as public transportation in the [city-state](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), and more [pedestrian-friendly](/wiki/Pedestrian_zone "Pedestrian zone") real estate development. On 29 November 2010, the coalition collapsed, resulting in [an election](/wiki/2011_Hamburg_state_election "2011 Hamburg state election") that was won by SPD.

Following the [Saarland state election](/wiki/2009_Saarland_state_election "2009 Saarland state election") of August 2009, The Greens held the balance of power after a close election where no two-party coalitions could create a stable [majority government](/wiki/Majority_government "Majority government"). After negotiations, the Saarland Greens rejected the option of a left-wing 'red-red-green' coalition with the SPD and [The Left](/wiki/The_Left_(Germany) "The Left (Germany)") (*Die Linke*) in order to form a centre-right state government with the CDU and [Free Democratic Party](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)") (FDP), a historical first time that a [Jamaica coalition](/wiki/Jamaica_coalition_(politics) "Jamaica coalition (politics)") has formed in German politics.

In June 2010, in the first state election following the victory of the [CDU/CSU](/wiki/CDU/CSU "CDU/CSU") and FDP in the [2009 federal election](/wiki/2009_German_federal_election "2009 German federal election"), the "black-yellow" CDU-FDP coalition in [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") under [Jürgen Rüttgers](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_R%C3%BCttgers "Jürgen Rüttgers") lost its majority. The Greens and the SPD came one seat short of a governing majority, but after multiple negotiations about coalitions of SPD and Greens with either the FDP or The Left, the SPD and Greens decided to form a [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government"),[[43]](#cite_note-44) which was possible because under the [constitution of North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/Constitution_of_North_Rhine-Westphalia "Constitution of North Rhine-Westphalia") a plurality of seats is sufficient to elect a [minister-president](/wiki/Minister-President "Minister-President").[[44]](#cite_note-45) So a red-green government in a state where it was defeated under [Peer Steinbrück](/wiki/Peer_Steinbr%C3%BCck "Peer Steinbrück") in 2005 came into office again on 14 June 2010 with the election of [Hannelore Kraft](/wiki/Hannelore_Kraft "Hannelore Kraft") as minister-president ([Cabinet Kraft I](/wiki/Cabinet_Kraft_I "Cabinet Kraft I")).

The Greens founded the first international chapter of a German political party in the U.S. on 13 April 2008 at the [Goethe-Institut](/wiki/Goethe-Institut "Goethe-Institut") in Washington D.C. Its main goal is "to provide a platform for politically active and green-oriented German citizens, in and beyond Washington D.C., to discuss and actively participate in German Green politics. [...] to foster professional and personal exchange, channeling the outcomes towards the political discourse in Germany."[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In March 2011 (two weeks after the [Fukushima nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster") had begun), the Greens made large gains in [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/2011_Rhineland-Palatinate_state_election "2011 Rhineland-Palatinate state election") and in [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/2011_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_state_election "2011 Baden-Württemberg state election"). In Baden-Württemberg they became the senior partner in a governing coalition for the first time. [Winfried Kretschmann](/wiki/Winfried_Kretschmann "Winfried Kretschmann") is now the first Green to serve as [Minister-President](/wiki/Minister-President "Minister-President") of a German State ([Cabinet Kretschmann I](/wiki/Cabinet_Kretschmann_I "Cabinet Kretschmann I") and [II](/wiki/Cabinet_Kretschmann_II "Cabinet Kretschmann II")). Polling data from August 2011 indicated that one in five Germans supported the Greens.[[46]](#cite_note-nytgrowth-47) From 4 October 2011 to [4 September 2016](/wiki/2016_Mecklenburg-Vorpommern_state_election "2016 Mecklenburg-Vorpommern state election"), the party was represented in all state parliaments.

Like the Social Democrats, the Greens backed Chancellor [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel") on most bailout votes in the German parliament during her second term, saying their pro-European stances overrode party politics.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Shortly before the elections, the party plummeted to a four-year low in the polls, undermining efforts by [Peer Steinbrück](/wiki/Peer_Steinbr%C3%BCck "Peer Steinbrück")'s Social Democrats to unseat Merkel.[[48]](#cite_note-49) While being in opposition on the federal level since 2005, the Greens have established themselves as a powerful force in Germany's political system. By 2016, the Greens had joined 11 out of 16 state governments in a variety of coalitions.[[49]](#cite_note-Jungjohann-2017-50) Over the years, they have built up an informal structure called G-coordination to organize interests between the federal party office, the parliamentary group in the Bundestag, and the Greens governing on the state level.[[49]](#cite_note-Jungjohann-2017-50)

The Greens remained the smallest of six parties in the Bundestag in the [2017 federal election](/wiki/2017_German_federal_election "2017 German federal election"), winning 8.9% of votes. After the election, they entered into talks for a [Jamaica coalition](/wiki/Jamaica_coalition_(politics) "Jamaica coalition (politics)") with the CDU and FDP. Discussions collapsed after the FDP withdrew in November.[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

After the federal election and unsuccessful Jamaica negotiations, the party held elections for two new co-leaders; incumbents Özdemir and Peter did not stand for re-election. [Robert Habeck](/wiki/Robert_Habeck "Robert Habeck") and [Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock") were elected with 81% and 64% of votes, respectively. Habeck had served as deputy premier and environment minister in [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein") since 2012, while Baerbock had been a leading figure in the party's Brandenburg branch since 2009. Their election was considered a break with tradition, as they were both members of the moderate wing.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

The Greens saw a major surge in support during the [Bavarian](/wiki/2018_Bavarian_state_election "2018 Bavarian state election") and [Hessian](/wiki/2018_Hessian_state_election "2018 Hessian state election") state elections in October 2018, becoming the second largest party in both.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55) They subsequently rose to second place behind the CDU/CSU in national polling, averaging between 17% and 20% over the next six months.[[55]](#cite_note-pollofpoll-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/2019_European_election_in_Germany_-_Greens_results.svg/250px-2019_European_election_in_Germany_-_Greens_results.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2019_European_election_in_Germany_-_Greens_results.svg)

A map showing the percentage of votes won by the Greens by district and state in the [2019 European Parliament election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_Germany "2019 European Parliament election in Germany"). Darker shades indicate a higher vote share.

In the [2019 European Parliament election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_Germany "2019 European Parliament election in Germany"), the Greens achieved their best ever result in a national election, placing second with 20.5% of the vote and winning 21 seats.[[56]](#cite_note-57) National polling released after the election showed a major boost for the party. The first poll after the election, conducted by Forsa, showed the Greens in first place on 27%. This was the first time the Greens had ever been in first place in a national opinion poll, and the first time in the history of the Federal Republic that any party other than the CDU/CSU or SPD had placed first in a national poll.[[57]](#cite_note-58) This trend continued as polls from May to July showed the CDU/CSU and Greens trading first place, after which point the CDU/CSU pulled ahead once more. The Greens continued to poll in the low 20% range into early 2020.[[55]](#cite_note-pollofpoll-56)

The Greens recorded best-ever results in the [Brandenburg](/wiki/2019_Brandenburg_state_election "2019 Brandenburg state election") (10.8%) and [Saxony](/wiki/2019_Saxony_state_election "2019 Saxony state election") (8.6%) state elections in September 2019, and subsequently joined coalition governments in both states.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60) They suffered an unexpected decline in the [Thuringian](/wiki/2019_Thuringian_state_election "2019 Thuringian state election") election in October, only narrowing retaining their seats with 5.2%. In the February [2020 Hamburg state election](/wiki/2020_Hamburg_state_election "2020 Hamburg state election"), the Greens became the second largest party, winning 24.2% of votes cast.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

In March 2021, the Greens improved their performance in [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/2021_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_state_election "2021 Baden-Württemberg state election"), where they remained the strongest party with 32.6% of votes, and [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/2021_Rhineland-Palatinate_state_election "2021 Rhineland-Palatinate state election"), where they moved into third place with 9.3%.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

Due to their sustained position as the second most popular party in national polling ahead of the [September 2021 federal election](/wiki/2021_German_federal_election "2021 German federal election"), the Greens chose to forgo the traditional dual lead-candidacy in favour of selecting a single Chancellor candidate.[[63]](#cite_note-64) Co-leader Annalena Baerbock was announced as Chancellor candidate on 19 April[[64]](#cite_note-65) and formally confirmed on 12 June with 98.5% approval.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/2021_German_federal_election_-_Greens_result.svg/250px-2021_German_federal_election_-_Greens_result.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2021_German_federal_election_-_Greens_result.svg)

A map showing the percentage of votes won by the Greens by constituency and state in the 2021 federal election. Darker shades indicate a higher vote share.

The Greens surged in opinion polls in late April and May, briefly surpassing the CDU as the most popular party in the country, but their numbers slipped back after Baerbock was caught up in several controversies. Her personal popularity also fell below that of both [Armin Laschet](/wiki/Armin_Laschet "Armin Laschet") and [Olaf Scholz](/wiki/Olaf_Scholz "Olaf Scholz"), the Chancellor candidates for the CDU and SPD, respectively. The party's fortunes did not reverse even after the [July floods](/wiki/2021_European_floods "2021 European floods"), which saw climate change return as the most important issue among voters.[[66]](#cite_note-67) The situation worsened in August as the SPD surged into first place to the detriment of both the CDU and Greens.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

### 2021–2025: Return to government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2021–2025: Return to government")]

The Greens finished in third place in the [2021 federal election](/wiki/2021_German_federal_election "2021 German federal election") with 14.8% of votes. Though their best ever federal election result, it was considered a bitter disappointment in light of their polling numbers during the previous three years.[[68]](#cite_note-69) They entered coalition talks with the FDP and SPD, eventually joining a [traffic light coalition](/wiki/Traffic_light_coalition "Traffic light coalition") under Chancellor [Olaf Scholz](/wiki/Olaf_Scholz "Olaf Scholz") which took office on 8 December 2021.[[69]](#cite_note-70) The Greens have five ministers in the [Scholz cabinet](/wiki/Scholz_cabinet "Scholz cabinet"), including Robert Habeck as Vice-Chancellor and Annalena Baerbock as foreign minister.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Since party statute mandates that party leaders may not hold government office, Baerbock and Habeck stepped down after entering cabinet. At a party conference in January 2022, [Ricarda Lang](/wiki/Ricarda_Lang "Ricarda Lang") and [Omid Nouripour](/wiki/Omid_Nouripour "Omid Nouripour") were elected to succeed them. At the time of her election, Lang was 28 years old, speaker for women's issues, and a former leader of the [Green Youth](/wiki/Green_Youth_(Germany) "Green Youth (Germany)"). 46-year-old Nouripour was foreign affairs spokesman and a member of the Bundestag since 2006. Of the new leaders, Lang is considered a representative of the party's left-wing, while Nouripour represents the right-wing.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Lang and Nouripour announced their resignations as party leaders in September 2024 after heavy defeats in the [Saxony](/wiki/2024_Saxony_state_election "2024 Saxony state election"), [Thuringia](/wiki/2024_Thuringian_state_election "2024 Thuringian state election") and [Brandenburg](/wiki/2024_Brandenburg_state_election "2024 Brandenburg state election") state elections that month, as well as the earlier [European Parliament election](/wiki/2024_European_Parliament_election_in_Germany "2024 European Parliament election in Germany"). In all three states, governing coalitions involving the Greens were not returned, and the party was wiped out in the latter two states while only narrowly retaining representation in Saxony. In the European elections, the Greens fell to fourth (behind the CDU/CSU, AfD and SPD) and lost 9 seats in the European parliament, falling to 12 seats. The party had fallen out of five state governments (additionally [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse")) since entering the federal governing coalition in 2021. Analysts pointed to its participation in the federal government requiring it to take stances that are contrary to its traditional clean-energy and pacifist ideals, as well as a stark collapse in support with young voters.[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

[Felix Banaszak](/wiki/Felix_Banaszak "Felix Banaszak") and [Franziska Brantner](/wiki/Franziska_Brantner "Franziska Brantner") elected as co-leaders in November 2024.[[75]](#cite_note-76) During the [2024 government crisis](/wiki/2024_German_government_crisis "2024 German government crisis"), in which the FDP left the traffic light coalition, the Greens stayed in a red-green minority coalition. The party abstained in the succeeding vote of no confidence, ensuring the government's collapse and the calling of the 2025 federal election.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### 2025–present: Return to opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2025–present: Return to opposition")]

The party chose incumbent [Vice Chancellor](/wiki/Vice-Chancellor_of_Germany "Vice-Chancellor of Germany") [Robert Habeck](/wiki/Robert_Habeck "Robert Habeck") as their [chancellor candidate](/wiki/Chancellor_candidate "Chancellor candidate") for the [2025 federal election](/wiki/2025_German_federal_election "2025 German federal election").[[77]](#cite_note-78) The Greens would go on to lose 33 seats in that election, garnering 11.6% of the vote.[[78]](#cite_note-79) The unpopularity of the Scholz cabinet has been credited with the result, although this was still the party's second best ever result.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The morning after the election, co-chancellor candidate of The Greens [Robert Habeck](/wiki/Robert_Habeck "Robert Habeck") announced his withdrawal from the party's leadership.[[80]](#cite_note-:30-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) The other co-candidate for The Greens, [Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock"), as well as former party leader [Ricarda Lang](/wiki/Ricarda_Lang "Ricarda Lang"), were expected to become the next party leaders in the Bundestag.[[80]](#cite_note-:30-81)

## Ideology and platform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ideology and platform")]

The party's main ideological trends are [green politics](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics") and [social liberalism](/wiki/Social_liberalism "Social liberalism").[[10]](#cite_note-Nordsieck-11)[[82]](#cite_note-left-liberal-83)[[83]](#cite_note-Filip-84) The party has also been described as [left-libertarian](/wiki/Left-libertarian "Left-libertarian"),[[84]](#cite_note-Kitschelt2004-85) and influenced by the [postmaterialist](/wiki/Postmaterialist "Postmaterialist") left.[[85]](#cite_note-Schmidt2002-86)[[86]](#cite_note-Jehlicka2003-87) The party's political position is generally described to be [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left "Centre-left");[[4]](#cite_note-Sloat-2020-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Aydin-Düzggit2012-5)[[6]](#cite_note-independentcentreleft-6)[[7]](#cite_note-euronewscentreleft-7) some describe the party as [centrist](/wiki/Centrist "Centrist").[[87]](#cite_note-Guardian_1-88)[[88]](#cite_note-MIR-89)[[89]](#cite_note-radicalism-90)[[90]](#cite_note-FT-91) The West German Greens played a crucial role in the development of green politics in Europe,[[91]](#cite_note-92) with their original program outlining "four principles: ecological, social, grassroots, and non-violent".[[92]](#cite_note-93) Initially ideologically heterogenous, the party took up a position on the radical left in its early years, which were dominated by conflicts between the more left-wing *Fundi* (fundamentalist) and more moderate *Realo* (realist) factions.

Writing in the late 1980s, [Sara Parkin](/wiki/Sara_Parkin "Sara Parkin") characterised the factional divides in the party as occurring along three axes: the strategic divide between fundis and realos, the [left–right political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum"), and the existence of [affinity groups](/wiki/Affinity_group "Affinity group"). She described the fundi perspective as one that stressed the importance of the green critique of existing developed industrial society and the Green's initial philosophy of being an "anti-party party" (in the words of fundi Petra Kelly) which was radically different from traditional political parties. From this view, watering down a radical worldview risked a failure to take sufficiently drastic action in the face of environmental crisis. As Kelly put it, "It does not help us in any way if we begin accepting lower and safer levels of, for example, radioactivity... We must speak out clear, loudly and courageously, if we know there are no safe levels".[[93]](#cite_note-parkin1234-94)

Fundis also express concern about losing touch with grassroots activists, and in Germany particularly emphasise solidarity with groups they consider to be victimised by the state, resulting in them being less likely to condemn use of violence by protest movements than realos.[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-doherty-96) According to Parkin, fundis were particularly prevalent on state and federal committees and executive bodies and formed a minority in parliamentary parties. Along with Kelly, other prominent fundis in the early years of the party included Rudolf Bahro (who colourfully described compromise as "cleaning the dragon's teeth and wiping away its excrement"), Jutta Ditfurth, [Lukas Beckmann](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lukas_Beckmann "de:Lukas Beckmann") and [Claudia Roth](/wiki/Claudia_Roth "Claudia Roth")[[93]](#cite_note-parkin1234-94)[[96]](#cite_note-97)[[97]](#cite_note-98) and the tendency also included the factional organisation [Left Forum](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linkes_Forum "de:Linkes Forum").[[95]](#cite_note-doherty-96)

Realos are [reformists](/wiki/Reformists "Reformists") who argue that the appropriate response to ecological crisis is to take action within the existing political system, and that to do otherwise is to condemn the green movement to irrelevance. They stress the importance of going beyond critique to develop practical solutions to environmental and other problems.[[98]](#cite_note-99) They additionally advocate for professionalisation of the party organisation, and believe in maintaining a certain distance from social movements.[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-digiacomo-101)

The most centrist realos like Joschka Fischer and Otto Schily were branded as "superrealos", and placed particular emphasis on moderating party positions to ensure that the party was a realistic potential coalition partner to the SPD, as well as questioning the party's early opposition to NATO, advocating outreach to [yuppies](/wiki/Yuppie "Yuppie") and [eco-capitalism](/wiki/Eco-capitalism "Eco-capitalism").[[101]](#cite_note-102) In Schily's case the development of his beliefs led him to leave the Greens for the SPD.[[102]](#cite_note-ely-103) Other realos like [Christa Vennegerts](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christa_Vennegerts "de:Christa Vennegerts") argued for clarification and development of policies in key areas such as economics and defence.[[103]](#cite_note-parkin126-104)

Other Greens associated with the realo faction include [Daniel Cohn-Bendit](/wiki/Daniel_Cohn-Bendit "Daniel Cohn-Bendit"), [Wolf-Dieter Hasenclever](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wolf-Dieter_Hasenclever "de:Wolf-Dieter Hasenclever"), [Waltraud Schoppe](/wiki/Waltraud_Schoppe "Waltraud Schoppe"), [Rezzo Schlauch](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rezzo_Schlauch "de:Rezzo Schlauch") and [Hubert Kleinert](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hubert_Kleinert "de:Hubert Kleinert"), as well as the thinktank the [Institute for Social-Ecological Research](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Institut_f%C3%BCr_sozial-%C3%B6kologische_Forschung "de:Institut für sozial-ökologische Forschung"). One policy area that has been of particular interest to realos is the area of work, with proposals such as shortening the working week and introduction of a [guaranteed minimum income](/wiki/Guaranteed_minimum_income "Guaranteed minimum income") as responses to structural economic changes in post-industrial capitalism, as advocated by realo-leaning social scientists including [Claus Offe](/wiki/Claus_Offe "Claus Offe") and [Helmut Wiesenthal](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helmut_Wiesenthal "de:Helmut Wiesenthal").[[15]](#cite_note-bondemann-16)[[102]](#cite_note-ely-103)[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106) According to Parkin realos constituted the majority of party members and elected officials.[[103]](#cite_note-parkin126-104)

In addition, during the 1980s a third intermediate faction emerged, labelled variously as "neutralos", "zentralos" or "aufbruch" (the latter after their manifesto *Aufbruch 88*) focused on preventing the conflict between fundis and realos from splitting the party and seeking to emphasise common ground.[[106]](#cite_note-107) This group was particularly associated with Antje Vollmer and stressed a stronger concentration on ecological issues.[[95]](#cite_note-doherty-96) Y. Michal Bondemann suggested that this faction reflected the development of a party bureaucracy, [cadre](/wiki/Cadre_(politics) "Cadre (politics)") and career politicians in the Greens.[[15]](#cite_note-bondemann-16)

In terms of the left-right spectrum, the left wing of the party was strengthened by an influx of radical left activists into the party when it was formed. Whilst some contributed to the development of new perspectives through dialogue with environmental activists, others hewed closer to their pre-existing ideology whilst within the party, such as the [Z Group](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gruppe_Z "de:Gruppe Z") which had originated as a breakaway from the Communist League. The Z Group was subject to a party tribunal in 1981 considering whether they should be expelled, which eventually decided against this. Prominent figures in this group included Thomas Ebermann and [Rainer Trampert](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rainer_Trampert "de:Rainer Trampert"). Ebermann and Trampert were both leftists and fundis, as was Jutta Ditfurth. Other more moderate [eco-socialists](/wiki/Eco-socialists "Eco-socialists") were aligned with the realos on strategic questions. The right of the party was weakened by the splitting of the [value conservative](/wiki/Value_conservatism "Value conservatism") wing led by Herbert Gruhl into the Ecological Democratic Party, but continued to be represented by a smaller eco-libertarian faction advocating a more [conservative liberal](/wiki/Conservative_liberal "Conservative liberal") approach to ecology which was particularly strong in Baden-Württemberg.[[107]](#cite_note-108) This faction, officially established in 1984, [opposed socialism](/wiki/Criticism_of_socialism "Criticism of socialism") and [social security](/wiki/Social_security "Social security").[[15]](#cite_note-bondemann-16)

These conflicts became less significant as the party moved toward the political mainstream in the 1990s.[[4]](#cite_note-Sloat-2020-4) This was mainly due to the weakening of the fundis leading to the realos establishing control of the party. In 1988 accusations of financial irregularities within the party were made by the news magazine *[Der Spiegel](/wiki/Der_Spiegel "Der Spiegel")* and also by Cohn-Bendit. Although an investigative commission rejected the claims in October, at party congress in Karlsruhe in December Ditfurth accused the realos of supplying false information to *Der Spiegel* in a speech which was poorly received by delegates. The congress passed a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") and the fundi controlled [Federal Executive Committee](/wiki/Federal_Executive_Committee_of_Alliance_90/The_Greens "Federal Executive Committee of Alliance 90/The Greens") resigned *en bloc*, resulting in the faction losing one of their biggest strongholds within the party.[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)

Realo control over the party was secured after its poor performance in the first federal election in the reunified Germany in 1990, when the West and [East German Greens](/wiki/East_German_Green_Party "East German Green Party") ran separately and the former failed to cross the electoral threshold and elected any Bundestag members.
