The Realos' position was also strengthened by the party's vote holding up in the [1991 state election in Hesse](/wiki/1991_Hessian_state_election "1991 Hessian state election"), a realo stronghold, opinion polling indicating that green voters supported a constructive approach regarding coalition formation and the influx of East German Greens, often with backgrounds in Protestant churches, which strengthened the realos and right wing of the party. As a result the realos were able to pass reforms at the 1991 party congress in [Neumünster](/wiki/Neum%C3%BCnster "Neumünster") which reflected their thinking, including reducing the number of party spokespersons from three to two, appointing a political director to lead management of the party and the establishing of a federal party council (*Bundesparteirat*) with members drawn from state parliamentary factions and party committees, tilting their composition towards the realos. This period also saw many fundis leave the party, including Trampert, Ebermann and Ditfurth (who together founded a new party, [Ecological Left](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%96kologische_Linke "de:Ökologische Linke")) and others in the eco-socialist stronghold of Hamburg: some turned to the reformed ex-ruling party in East Germany, the [Party of Democratic Socialism](/wiki/Party_of_Democratic_Socialism_(Germany) "Party of Democratic Socialism (Germany)"), including Left Forum.[[95]](#cite_note-doherty-96)[[102]](#cite_note-ely-103)[[110]](#cite_note-111)[[111]](#cite_note-112) [Paul Hockenos](/wiki/Paul_Hockenos "Paul Hockenos") has suggested that this period saw the end of the fundi-realo dimension of struggle within the party, although left-right contestation continued.[[112]](#cite_note-113) After this period, the party moved away from pacifism, supporting EU military cooperation and military interventions in [Yugoslavia](/wiki/NATO_bombing_of_Yugoslavia "NATO bombing of Yugoslavia") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), and watered down its commitment to other non-environmental issues such as feminism and social justice.[[100]](#cite_note-digiacomo-101)

During the 2021 federal election, the [WZB Berlin Social Science Center](/wiki/WZB_Berlin_Social_Science_Center "WZB Berlin Social Science Center") classified the party as the most centrist of Germany's left-wing parties.[[113]](#cite_note-Deutsche_Welle-2021-114) [Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock") campaigned from the left of the SPD, stating that the party's economic program is geared towards the "common good" while the SPD's no longer is.[[114]](#cite_note-115) The party has a more pragmatic approach to workers' rights than the SPD.[[4]](#cite_note-Sloat-2020-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Aydin-Düzggit2012-5)[[113]](#cite_note-Deutsche_Welle-2021-114) On the other hand, the party clearly holds positions to the left of the SPD on issues such as fiscal discipline,[[115]](#cite_note-116) particularly on the [debt brake](/wiki/Debt_brake_(Germany) "Debt brake (Germany)"),[[116]](#cite_note-117) the climate transition,[[117]](#cite_note-118) and [property expropriation in Berlin](/wiki/2021_Berlin_referendum "2021 Berlin referendum").[[118]](#cite_note-119) They are focusing on environmentalist and socially [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism") policies.[[119]](#cite_note-Grüll-2021-120) Emphasis is placed on mitigating [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), reducing [carbon emissions](/wiki/Carbon_emissions "Carbon emissions"), and fostering [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") and environmentally-friendly practices.[[120]](#cite_note-121) They support equality, [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"), and humanitarian responses to events such as the [European migrant crisis](/wiki/European_migrant_crisis "European migrant crisis").[[121]](#cite_note-122) Their fiscal platform is flexible and seeks to balance social, economic, and environmental interests.[[122]](#cite_note-Höhne-2018-123) The party is strongly [pro-European](/wiki/Pro-European "Pro-European"), advocating [European federalism](/wiki/European_federalism "European federalism"),[[123]](#cite_note-Für_eine_europäische_Republik-124) and promotes wider international cooperation, including strengthening existing alliances.[[122]](#cite_note-Höhne-2018-123)

Starting from the leadership of [Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock") and [Robert Habeck](/wiki/Robert_Habeck "Robert Habeck"), commentators have observed the Greens taking a pragmatic, moderate approach to work with parties from across the political spectrum. Baerbock described their stances and style as a form of "radical realism" attempting to reconcile principles with practical politics.[[122]](#cite_note-Höhne-2018-123)[[124]](#cite_note-125) At the same time, the party has denounced [populism](/wiki/Populism "Populism") and division, and placed rhetorical emphasis on optimism and cross-party cooperation.[[4]](#cite_note-Sloat-2020-4)[[125]](#cite_note-126) Accompanied by record high popularity and election results, this led some to suggest that the Greens were filling a gap in the political centre, which was left by the declining popularity of the CDU/CSU and SPD.[[4]](#cite_note-Sloat-2020-4)[[119]](#cite_note-Grüll-2021-120)

### Economic policies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economic policies")]

The party has economically [left-liberal](/wiki/Left-liberal "Left-liberal") views.[[82]](#cite_note-left-liberal-83)

### Foreign policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Foreign policy")]

The Greens are regarded as taking a [Atlanticist](/wiki/Atlanticist "Atlanticist") line on defense and pushing for a stronger common EU foreign policy,[[126]](#cite_note-127) especially against Russia and China.[[127]](#cite_note-Germany-128)[[128]](#cite_note-129) Green Party co-leader [Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock") has proposed a post-[pacifist](/wiki/Pacifism "Pacifism") foreign policy.[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-131) She supports [eastward expansion of NATO](/wiki/Enlargement_of_NATO "Enlargement of NATO")[[127]](#cite_note-Germany-128) and has considered the number of [UN resolutions](/wiki/List_of_United_Nations_resolutions_concerning_Israel "List of United Nations resolutions concerning Israel") critical of Israel as "absurd compared to resolutions against other states."[[131]](#cite_note-132) The party's program included references to [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") as an "indispensable" part of European security.[[132]](#cite_note-133) The Greens have promised to abolish the contested [Nord Stream 2](/wiki/Nord_Stream_2 "Nord Stream 2") pipeline to ship Russian natural gas to Germany.[[133]](#cite_note-134) The party criticized the EU's [investment deal with China](/wiki/Comprehensive_Agreement_on_Investment "Comprehensive Agreement on Investment").[[134]](#cite_note-135) It describes China as a "systematic rival", though the party also favors cooperation with China to fight climate change.[[135]](#cite_note-136) In 2016, the Greens criticised Germany's defense plan with [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), which has been [waging war](/wiki/Saudi_Arabian-led_intervention_in_Yemen "Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen") in Yemen and has been accused of massive [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Saudi_Arabia "Human rights in Saudi Arabia") violations.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

The party remains divided over issues such as [nuclear disarmament](/wiki/Nuclear_disarmament "Nuclear disarmament") and [U.S. nuclear weapons on German territory](/wiki/Germany_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "Germany and weapons of mass destruction"). Some Greens want Germany to sign the United Nations' [Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140) About the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), the [Federal Minister of Food and Agriculture](/wiki/Federal_Ministry_of_Food_and_Agriculture_(Germany) "Federal Ministry of Food and Agriculture (Germany)") [Cem Özdemir](/wiki/Cem_%C3%96zdemir "Cem Özdemir") (former president of the party) criticized Swedish climate activist [Greta Thunberg](/wiki/Greta_Thunberg "Greta Thunberg") for her presence and support of pro-Palestinian demonstrations in Berlin, calling on everyone to reconsider their opinions about her.[[140]](#cite_note-141)[[141]](#cite_note-142)

### Energy and nuclear power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Energy and nuclear power")]

See also: [Anti-nuclear movement in Germany](/wiki/Anti-nuclear_movement_in_Germany "Anti-nuclear movement in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Gr%C3%BCne_protests_against_nuclear_energy.jpg/250px-Gr%C3%BCne_protests_against_nuclear_energy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gr%C3%BCne_protests_against_nuclear_energy.jpg)

Anti-nuclear protest near nuclear waste disposal centre at Gorleben in northern Germany, on 8 November 2008

Ever since the party's inception, The Greens have been concerned with the immediate halt of construction or operation of all nuclear power stations. As an alternative, they promote a shift to non-nuclear [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") and a comprehensive program of [energy conservation](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation").[[142]](#cite_note-nuke-143)

In 1986, large parts of Germany were covered with [radioactive contamination](/wiki/Radioactive_contamination "Radioactive contamination") from the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") and Germans went to great lengths to deal with the contamination. Germany's anti-nuclear stance was strengthened. From the mid-1990s onwards, anti-nuclear protests were primarily directed against transports of [radioactive waste](/wiki/High-level_radioactive_waste_management "High-level radioactive waste management") in ["CASTOR" containers](/wiki/Dry_cask_storage "Dry cask storage").

After the Chernobyl disaster, the Greens became more radicalised and resisted compromise on the nuclear issue. During the 1990s, a re-orientation towards a moderate program occurred, with concern about [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") and [ozone depletion](/wiki/Ozone_depletion "Ozone depletion") taking a more prominent role. During the federal red-green government (1998–2005) many people[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] became disappointed with what they saw as excessive compromise on key Greens policies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Atom-Moratorium.svg/250px-Atom-Moratorium.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Atom-Moratorium.svg)

Eight German nuclear power reactors (Biblis A and B, Brunsbuettel, Isar 1, Kruemmel, Neckarwestheim 1, Philippsburg 1 and Unterweser) were declared permanently shut down on 6 August 2011, following the Japanese [Fukushima nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima nuclear disaster").[[143]](#cite_note-144)

### Environment and climate policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Environment and climate policy")]

The central idea of green politics is [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development").[[144]](#cite_note-145) The concept of environmental protection is the cornerstone of Alliance 90/The Greens policy. In particular, the economic, energy and transport policy claims are in close interaction with environmental considerations. The Greens acknowledge the natural environment as a high priority and animal protection should be enshrined as a national objective in constitutional law. An effective environmental policy would be based on a common environmental code, with the urgent integration of a climate change bill. During the red-green coalition (1998–2005) a policy of agricultural change was launched labeled as a paradigm shift in agricultural policy towards a more ecological friendly agriculture, which needs to continue.

The Greens have praised the [European Green Deal](/wiki/European_Green_Deal "European Green Deal"), which aims to make the EU [climate neutral](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") by 2050. Climate change is at the center of all policy considerations. This includes environmental policy and safety and social aspects. The plans of the Alliance 90/The Greens provide a climate change bill laying down binding reductions to greenhouse gas emissions in Germany by 2020 restricting emissions to minus 40 percent compared to 1990.

### European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: European Union")]

Alliance 90/The Greens supports the eventual [federalization of the European Union](/wiki/Federalisation_of_the_European_Union "Federalisation of the European Union") into a Federal European Republic (German: *Föderale Europäische Republik*), i.e. a single [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") European [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").[[123]](#cite_note-Für_eine_europäische_Republik-124)[[145]](#cite_note-146)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transport")]

A similarly high priority is given to transport policy. The switch from a traveling allowance to a mobility allowance, which is paid regardless of income to all employees, replacing company car privileges. The truck toll will act as a climate protection instrument internalizing the external costs of transport. Railway should be promoted in order to achieve the desired environmental objectives and the comprehensive care of customers. The railway infrastructure is to remain permanently in the public sector, allowing a reduction in expenditure on road construction infrastructure. The Greens want to control privileges on [kerosene](/wiki/Kerosene "Kerosene") and for international flights, introduce an air ticket levy.

Fossil fuels such as heavy oil or diesel shall be replaced by emission-neutral fuels and green propulsion systems in order to make shipping climate-neutral in the long term.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

### Social policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Social policy")]

For many years, the Green Party has advocated against the "Ehegattensplitting" policy, under which the incomes of married couples are split for taxation purposes. Furthermore, the Party advocates for a massive increase in federal spending for places in preschools, and for increased investment in education: an additional 1 billion Euros for vocational schools and 200 million Euros more BAföG (Bundesausbildungsförderungsgesetz in German, approximately translated to "the Federal Law for the Advancement of Education") for adults.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

In its 2013 platform, the Green Party successfully advocated for a minimum wage of 8.50 Euro per hour, which was implemented on 1 January 2015.[[148]](#cite_note-149) It continues to press for higher minimum wages.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

The Greens want the starting retirement age to remain 67,[[150]](#cite_note-151) but with some qualifications – for example, a provision for partial retirement.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[151]](#cite_note-152)

The party supports and has supported various forms of [rent regulation](/wiki/Rent_control "Rent control").[[152]](#cite_note-153) During the 2021 election, the party called for rent hikes to be capped at 2.5% per year.[[153]](#cite_note-154)

The Greens support [progressive taxation](/wiki/Progressive_taxation "Progressive taxation") and is critical of FDP efforts to cut taxes for top earners.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

### Women and LGBTQIA+ rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Women and LGBTQIA+ rights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/2021-09-12_Politik%2C_TV-Triell_Bundestagswahl_2021_1DX_3651_by_Stepro.jpg/250px-2021-09-12_Politik%2C_TV-Triell_Bundestagswahl_2021_1DX_3651_by_Stepro.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021-09-12_Politik,_TV-Triell_Bundestagswahl_2021_1DX_3651_by_Stepro.jpg)

Green supporters supporting Annalena Baerbock in 2021

The Green Party supports the implementation of quotas in executive boards, the policy of equal pay for equal work, and continuing the fight against domestic violence.[[155]](#cite_note-156) According to its website, the Green Party "fights for the acceptance and against the exclusion of homosexuals, bisexuals, intersex- and transgender people and others".[[156]](#cite_note-157)

In order to recognize the political persecution that [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT")+ people face abroad, the Green Party wants to extend asylum to LGBTQIA+ people abroad.[[157]](#cite_note-158) The policy change was sponsored primarily by [Volker Beck](/wiki/Volker_Beck_(politician) "Volker Beck (politician)"), one of the Party's most prominent gay members.[[158]](#cite_note-Politik_unterm_Regenbogen-159) Because of the extensive support the Green Party has given the LGBTQIA+ community since its conception, many LGBTQIA+ people vote for the Green Party even if their political ideology does not quite align otherwise.[[158]](#cite_note-Politik_unterm_Regenbogen-159)

### Drug policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Drug policy")]

The party supports the legalization and regulation of [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") and was the sponsor of the 2015 proposed iteration of the [German cannabis control bill](/wiki/German_cannabis_control_bill "German cannabis control bill"). Furthermore, the Greens support research on the drug and the use of marijuana for medicinal purposes.[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161)

## Electorate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Electorate")]

A 2000 study by the Infratest Dimap political research company has suggested the Green voter demographic includes those on higher incomes (e.g. above €2,000/month) and the party's support is less among households with lower incomes. The same [polling research](/wiki/Opinion_poll "Opinion poll") also concluded that the Greens received fewer votes from the unemployed and general working population, with business people favouring the party as well as the centre-right liberal [Free Democratic Party](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)"). According to the Infratest Dimap political research company for the [ARD](/wiki/ARD_(broadcaster) "ARD (broadcaster)"), the Greens received more voters from the age group 34–42 than any other age group and that the young were generally more supportive of the party than the old.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

The Greens have a higher voter demographic in urban areas than rural areas, except for a small number of rural areas with pressing local environmental concerns, such as [strip mining](/wiki/Strip_mining "Strip mining") or [radioactive waste](/wiki/Radioactive_waste "Radioactive waste") deposits. The cities of [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), Berlin, [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), Frankfurt and [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") have among the highest percentages of Green voters in the country. Large towns with Green mayors as of March 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alliance_90/The_Greens&action=edit) include [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") and [Münster](/wiki/M%C3%BCnster "Münster").[[162]](#cite_note-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[165]](#cite_note-166) The party has a lower level of support in the states of the former [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") (East Germany); nonetheless, the party is currently represented in every state [Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag") except [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), [Saarland](/wiki/Landtag_of_Saarland "Landtag of Saarland"), and [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia").
